title: Trimming rabbit claws
subtitle: How to restrain a rabbit, identify the quick, and trim overgrown claws safely.
excerpt: Rabbits that live on soft bedding or grass do not wear their claws down through normal movement. Overgrown claws catch on housing mesh, change the posture of the foot, and can curve back into the pad. Trimming takes five minutes per rabbit once learned, but the restraint technique matters: a rabbit that struggles during claw trimming can break its own spine.
sourceNotes: RSPCA: rabbit care guidelines. British Rabbit Council: husbandry standards for show and breed rabbits. Varga (2013), Textbook of Rabbit Medicine (2nd ed.), Butterworth-Heinemann: handling and grooming chapter.

glossaryTerms.definition: The blood vessel and nerve that runs through the centre of a rabbit claw. In white or pale claws it is visible as a pink line. In dark or black claws it cannot be seen directly. Cutting into the quick causes pain and bleeding. A styptic pencil or corn flour pressed against the cut end stops minor bleeding within a minute.

A state of temporary immobility that some rabbits enter when placed on their back. Sometimes called trancing or hypnotising. The rabbit appears calm but is in a high-fear state. Using trance for claw trimming is discouraged by welfare bodies because it induces fear regardless of appearance. A correctly restrained alert rabbit is safer and less stressful for the animal.

content.text: Rabbit claw trimming needs two things: a rabbit that is restrained firmly enough to stay still, and the ability to identify
before each cut. Both are learnable in one session. The main hazard is not the claw trimming itself but a rabbit that kicks violently while being held, which is how spinal fractures happen.

. Place the rabbit on a firm, non-slip surface such as a rubber mat on a table. Wrap the rabbit's body against your own body with one arm, supporting the full weight of the hindquarters. The rabbit's back must be fully supported at all times. A rabbit that kicks with its back legs when the hindquarters are unsupported generates enough force to fracture the lumbar spine. Work on one foot at a time, tucking the others against the body.
For a rabbit that is very fidgety, wrap it in a towel burrito-style, exposing one foot at a time. This reduces the kicking risk significantly.

Hold the foot up against a bright light or use a small torch shone through the claw from behind. In pale claws, the quick appears as a pink cone extending from the base of the claw. Aim to cut 2 to 3 mm below the tip of the quick, leaving a visible margin of white or pale claw below the pink.

Dark claws need more caution. Trim a very small amount at a time. As you cut progressively through the tip, a dark clipped surface will appear initially; as you approach the quick the cut surface changes to show a pale or slightly grey moist disc. Stop as soon as you see this change.

Press a dry tissue or small amount of corn flour against the claw end and hold it there for 30 to 60 seconds. The bleeding usually stops within a minute. The rabbit will show discomfort but the injury is minor and heals quickly. Cutting the quick does not mean the claw trimming was badly done; it means you went fractionally too far. Adjust your margin on subsequent claws.
